examples.explanation: Compute cost for range [0,2]: Try root=10: left=[], right=[12,20]. Cost = sum(freq[0..2])=92 + cost([])=0 + cost([12,20]). For [12,20]: Try root=12: left=[], right=[20]. Cost = sum(freq[1..2])=58 + 0 + cost([20]). For [20]: Cost = 50. Total=58+50=108. Try root=20: left=[12], right=[]. Cost = 58 + cost([12]) + 0. For [12]: Cost = 8. Total=58+8=66. Min=66. So cost([12,20])=66. Total for root=10: 92+0+66=158. Try root=12: left=[10], right=[20]. Cost = 92 + cost([10]) + cost([20]). cost([10])=34, cost([20])=50. Total=92+34+50=176. Try root=20: left=[10,12], right=[]. Cost = 92 + cost([10,12]) + 0. For [10,12]: Try root=10: left=[], right=[12]. Cost = sum(freq[0..1])=42 + 0 + cost([12])=8. Total=50. Try root=12: left=[10], right=[]. Cost = 42 + cost([10])=34 + 0 = 76. Min=50. So cost([10,12])=50. Total for root=20: 92+50+0=142. Minimum among roots: min(158,176,142)=142.

Compute cost for range [0,1]: Try root=10: left=[], right=[12]. Cost = sum(freq[0..1])=42 + cost([])=0 + cost([12]). cost([12])=8. Total=42+0+8=50. Try root=12: left=[10], right=[]. Cost = 42 + cost([10]) + 0. cost([10])=34. Total=42+34+0=76. Minimum=50.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(n * 2^n)
approaches.summary: Recursively try all possible BST structures by selecting each key as the root and computing the cost based on subtree frequencies. Use it for small n to understand the problem, but it's impractical due to exponential time.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursion for Optimal BST
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution trying all possible BST structures to find minimum search cost.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the Optimal BST problem by recursively exploring every possible binary search tree configuration. The intuition is that for any subtree containing keys from index i to j, we can try each key k in that range as the root. When key k is the root, keys i to k-1 form the left subtree and keys k+1 to j form the right subtree. The cost of a subtree is the sum of frequencies of all keys in that subtree (since each search goes through the root) plus the costs of the left and right subtrees. We recursively compute this for all possible roots and take the minimum. The base case occurs when i > j (empty subtree), which has cost 0. The time complexity is O(n * 2^n) because for n keys, there are Catalan number (approximately 4^n/(n^(3/2)*√π)) possible BST structures, and we compute cost for each. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack. This approach connects to dynamic programming solutions by revealing the optimal substructure property. Common mistakes include incorrect frequency summation (must include all keys in subtree, not just root) and off-by-one errors in recursive calls. Real code pattern uses nested loops to try each root and recursive calls for subtrees.

examples.trace: Compute cost for range [0,2]: Try root=10: left=[], right=[12,20]. Cost = sum(freq[0..2])=92 + cost([])=0 + cost([12,20]). For [12,20]: Try root=12: left=[], right=[20]. Cost = sum(freq[1..2])=58 + 0 + cost([20]). For [20]: Cost = 50. Total=58+50=108. Try root=20: left=[12], right=[]. Cost = 58 + cost([12]) + 0. For [12]: Cost = 8. Total=58+8=66. Min=66. So cost([12,20])=66. Total for root=10: 92+0+66=158. Try root=12: left=[10], right=[20]. Cost = 92 + cost([10]) + cost([20]). cost([10])=34, cost([20])=50. Total=92+34+50=176. Try root=20: left=[10,12], right=[]. Cost = 92 + cost([10,12]) + 0. For [10,12]: Try root=10: left=[], right=[12]. Cost = sum(freq[0..1])=42 + 0 + cost([12])=8. Total=50. Try root=12: left=[10], right=[]. Cost = 42 + cost([10])=34 + 0 = 76. Min=50. So cost([10,12])=50. Total for root=20: 92+50+0=142. Minimum among roots: min(158,176,142)=142.

Compute cost for range [0,1]: Try root=10: left=[], right=[12]. Cost = sum(freq[0..1])=42 + cost([])=0 + cost([12]). cost([12])=8. Total=42+0+8=50. Try root=12: left=[10], right=[]. Cost = 42 + cost([10]) + 0. cost([10])=34. Total=42+34+0=76. Minimum=50.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Cost Calculation for a Subtree
ladders.desc: Helper function that computes minimum cost for a contiguous range of keys.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function that calculates the minimum search cost for a subtree defined by keys from index i to j. The intuition is that any BST for this range has one root key, with left and right subtrees covering the remaining keys. By trying each possible root, we can find the optimal structure. The algorithm: If i > j, return 0 (empty subtree). Otherwise, initialize min_cost to infinity. For each possible root k from i to j: Compute sum of frequencies from i to j (this represents the cost contribution of the root level for all keys in this subtree). Recursively compute cost for left subtree (keys i to k-1) and right subtree (keys k+1 to j). Total cost = sum + left_cost + right_cost. Update min_cost if this is smaller. Return min_cost. Time complexity for this function on n keys is O(n * C(n)) where C(n) is Catalan number, but practically we analyze per call: O(n) for the sum computation and loop, with exponential recursive calls. Space: O(n) recursion depth. This feeds into L0 by being called for the full range [0, n-1]. Common mistakes: Forgetting to include the sum of all frequencies in the current subtree (not just the root's frequency) and incorrect base case handling. Real code uses memoization to avoid recomputation in practice.

examples.trace: Call for [0,2]: i=0, j=2. Not base case. min_cost=∞. Try k=0: sum=freq[0]+freq[1]+freq[2]=34+8+50=92. left=recursive(1,2), right=recursive(3,2)=0. For left(1,2): Try k=1: sum=8+50=58, left(2,2), right(3,2)=0. For left(2,2): sum=50, left(3,2)=0, right(3,2)=0, cost=50. So left(1,2) cost=58+0+50=108. Try k=2: sum=58, left(1,1), right(3,2)=0. left(1,1): sum=8, cost=8. So cost=58+8+0=66. Min for left(1,2)=66. So for k=0: cost=92+66+0=158. Try k=1: sum=92, left(0,0), right(2,2). left(0,0): sum=34, cost=34. right(2,2): sum=50, cost=50. Total=92+34+50=176. Try k=2: sum=92, left(0,1), right(3,2)=0. left(0,1): Try k=0: sum=34+8=42, left(1,1)=8, right(2,1)=0, cost=50. Try k=1: sum=42, left(0,0)=34, right(2,1)=0, cost=76. Min=50. So cost=92+50+0=142. min_cost=min(158,176,142)=142.

Call for [1,2]: i=1, j=2. min_cost=∞. Try k=1: sum=freq[1]+freq[2]=8+50=58. left=recursive(2,1)=0, right=recursive(3,2)=0. For right(3,2): base case (i=3>j=2) return 0. So cost=58+0+0=58? Wait, need recursive for right: actually right is recursive(2,2). Let's correct: For k=1: left=recursive(2,1)=0 (since i=2>j=1), right=recursive(3,2)=0 (i=3>j=2). So cost=58+0+0=58. Try k=2: sum=58, left=recursive(1,1), right=recursive(3,2)=0. left(1,1): sum=8, cost=8. So cost=58+8+0=66. min_cost=min(58,66)=58? But earlier example gave 66. Let's recompute carefully: For range [1,2], keys 12 and 20. If root=12: cost = (8+50) + cost(left empty) + cost(right with 20). cost(right with 20) = 50. So total=58+0+50=108. If root=20: cost = (8+50) + cost(left with 12) + cost(right empty). cost(left with 12)=8. So total=58+8+0=66. Minimum is 66. My trace had error: For k=1, right should be recursive(2,2) not recursive(3,2). Correct: k=1: left=recursive(2,1)=0, right=recursive(2,2). recursive(2,2): sum=50, cost=50. So cost=58+0+50=108. k=2: left=recursive(1,1), right=recursive(3,2)=0. recursive(1,1): sum=8, cost=8. So cost=58+8+0=66. min=66.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Trying All Roots and Combining Subtree Costs
ladders.desc: The technique of enumerating each key as root and recursively solving left/right subtrees.
ladders.explanation: This core logic is the heart of the brute force approach: for any set of keys, the optimal BST can be found by considering every key as the potential root. The intuition stems from the BST property: all keys in the left subtree are smaller than the root, and all in the right subtree are larger. Given sorted keys, if we pick a root at index k, then keys i..k-1 must go to the left, and keys k+1..j to the right. The cost of the tree with root k is the sum of frequencies of all keys in the range (since each search goes through the root) plus the optimal costs of the left and right subtrees. By trying all k, we guarantee finding the minimum. The algorithm: Loop k from i to j, compute cost for each, take min. This exhibits optimal substructure: optimal solution to the full problem contains optimal solutions to subproblems. Time: For n keys, there are n choices for root, and each leads to two recursive calls, leading to exponential complexity. Space: Recursion depth up to n. This feeds into L1 by being the main loop within the recursive function. Common mistakes: Incorrectly limiting k range or mishandling empty subtrees. Real code uses this loop inside recursion.

examples.trace: For range [10,12,20]: Try root=10 (k=0): cost = sum(34+8+50)=92 + cost(left []) + cost(right [12,20]). cost(right [12,20]) computed as: try root=12: cost=58 + cost(left []) + cost(right [20])=58+0+50=108; try root=20: cost=58 + cost(left [12]) + cost(right [])=58+8+0=66; min=66. So total=92+0+66=158. Try root=12 (k=1): cost=92 + cost(left [10]) + cost(right [20]). cost(left [10])=34, cost(right [20])=50. Total=92+34+50=176. Try root=20 (k=2): cost=92 + cost(left [10,12]) + cost(right []). cost(left [10,12]): try root=10: cost=42 + cost(left []) + cost(right [12])=42+0+8=50; try root=12: cost=42 + cost(left [10]) + cost(right [])=42+34+0=76; min=50. Total=92+50+0=142. Minimum cost=142 with root=20.

For range [10,12]: Try root=10 (k=0): cost = sum(34+8)=42 + cost(left []) + cost(right [12]). cost(right [12])=8. Total=42+0+8=50. Try root=12 (k=1): cost=42 + cost(left [10]) + cost(right []). cost(left [10])=34. Total=42+34+0=76. Minimum cost=50 with root=10.

ladders.title: Building Block — Computing Sum of Frequencies for a Range
ladders.desc: Calculating the total frequency of keys in a contiguous index range.
ladders.explanation: This building block computes the sum of frequencies for keys from index i to j, inclusive. In the Optimal BST problem, this sum represents the contribution to the search cost from the root level for that subtree: every search for a key in this subtree will pass through the root, so the frequency of each key is added once. The intuition is that the cost of a tree is hierarchical; at each level, we add the frequencies of all keys in the subtree. The algorithm is straightforward: initialize total=0, loop from i to j, add freq[k] to total. Alternatively, use prefix sums for efficiency. Time complexity: O(j-i+1) per call, which in brute force can be O(n) per recursive call. Space: O(1). This feeds into L2 by being used in the cost calculation for each root choice. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in loop bounds or incorrect indexing. Real code often precomputes prefix sums to reduce time.

examples.trace: total=0. k=1: total=0+8=8. k=2: total=8+50=58.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Array Elements and Simple Addition
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of reading values from arrays and adding numbers.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves accessing elements in an array (or list) using indices and performing addition. In the context of Optimal BST, we need to read frequency values from the freq array and sum them. The intuition is that data is stored in arrays for efficient access, and addition is used to accumulate totals. The algorithm: To access, use syntax like freq[i] to get the value at index i. To add, use total += freq[i]. This is foundational because all cost calculations rely on summing frequencies. Time: O(1) per access and addition. Space: O(1). This feeds into L3 as the core of computing range sums. Common mistakes: Index out of bounds (e.g., accessing beyond array length) or incorrect variable initialization. Real code ensures loops stay within valid indices.

examples.trace: Initialize total=0. Access freq[0]=34, total=0+34=34. Access freq[1]=8, total=34+8=42. Access freq[2]=50, total=42+50=92.
total=0. Access freq[0]=10, total=10. Access freq[1]=20, total=30.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Understanding how to store and access data in arrays using indices.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers arrays (or lists in Python), which are data structures that store elements in contiguous memory locations, accessible via indices. In programming, an array allows you to hold multiple values of the same type. Indexing starts at 0 in most languages, meaning the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, etc. The intuition is that arrays provide efficient, O(1) access to any element if you know its index. For the Optimal BST problem, we use arrays to store keys and frequencies, where the index corresponds to the position in sorted order. The algorithm: Declare an array, e.g., freq = [34, 8, 50]. Access elements with freq[0] for 34, freq[1] for 8. Ensure indices are within bounds: from 0 to length-1. Time: O(1) for access. Space: O(n) to store n elements. This feeds into L4 as the basis for reading values. Common mistakes: Using 1-based indexing or accessing out-of-range indices. Real code uses loops to iterate through arrays safely.

examples.trace: The array has elements: index 0: 34, index 1: 8, index 2: 50. Accessing index 1 gives the second element, which is 8.
keys array: index 0: 10, index 1: 12, index 2: 20. Access index 0: 10. Access index 2: 20.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming O(n^3)
approaches.summary: Use DP to store minimum cost for subtrees defined by indices i and j, iterating over all possible roots. Apply it for moderate n where brute force fails, but it's slower than optimized DP.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Compute Minimum Search Cost for Optimal BST
ladders.desc: Given keys and frequencies, compute the minimum search cost for an optimal binary search tree using O(n³) dynamic programming.
ladders.explanation: The optimal BST problem aims to construct a binary search tree that minimizes the total search cost, where each key has an associated search frequency. The total cost is the sum over all keys of (depth of key + 1) × frequency, since each comparison at a node contributes to the cost. The DP approach solves this by considering all possible subtrees. We define dp[i][j] as the minimum cost for a BST containing keys from index i to j (inclusive). To compute dp[i][j], we consider each key k in [i, j] as the root. The cost of the subtree with root k is the sum of: 1) The cost of the left subtree (dp[i][k-1]), 2) The cost of the right subtree (dp[k+1][j]), and 3) The sum of frequencies from i to j (since making k the root adds one level to all keys in the subtree, increasing their cost by their frequencies). We iterate over all k to find the minimum. The base case is when i > j (empty subtree), where cost is 0. We also precompute prefix sums of frequencies to quickly get the sum from i to j. The algorithm fills the DP table for increasing lengths of intervals, starting from length 0 (single key) up to length n-1 (all keys). The final answer is dp[0][n-1]. Intuition: By trying all possible roots, we ensure we consider all BST structures, and DP avoids recomputation by storing optimal costs for subtrees. Time complexity is O(n³) due to three nested loops (interval length, start index, root index). Space complexity is O(n²) for the DP table. Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of computing dp[i][j] by iterating over roots. Common mistakes include incorrect base cases (e.g., handling i > j), forgetting to add the sum of frequencies, or misindexing when accessing dp for left/right subtrees. Real code pattern: Initialize dp as n×n matrix with zeros, precompute prefix sums. For length from 0 to n-1, for i from 0 to n-length-1, set j = i+length, set dp[i][j] to infinity, for k from i to j, compute cost = dp[i][k-1] + dp[k+1][j] + sum(freq[i:j+1]), update minimum. Return dp[0][n-1].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute DP[i][j] for a Subtree Interval
ladders.desc: Given DP table and prefix sums, compute the minimum cost for BST over keys from i to j by trying all roots.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the optimal BST DP algorithm. For a fixed interval [i, j] of keys, we need to find the minimum search cost for a BST containing exactly those keys. The idea is to consider each key k in the interval as a potential root. If k is the root, then the left subtree contains keys [i, k-1] and the right subtree contains keys [k+1, j]. The cost of the subtree with root k is the sum of: 1) The optimal cost of the left subtree (dp[i][k-1]), 2) The optimal cost of the right subtree (dp[k+1][j]), and 3) The sum of frequencies from i to j (denoted sumFreq(i, j)). The third term accounts for the fact that making k the root adds one level to all keys in the interval, increasing their contribution to the total cost by their frequencies. We iterate over all k from i to j, compute this cost, and take the minimum as dp[i][j]. The base case is when i > j (empty subtree), where the cost is 0. In implementation, we handle this by checking bounds: if k-1 < i, treat left as empty (cost 0); similarly for right. We use precomputed prefix sums to get sumFreq(i, j) in O(1) time. Intuition: By trying all roots, we explore all possible BST structures for the interval, and the additive nature of the cost allows us to combine subtree costs. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(length) where length = j-i+1, as we loop over k. Space is O(1) extra. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each interval in the main DP filling loop. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in accessing dp for left/right subtrees, incorrect handling of empty subtrees, or forgetting to add sumFreq. Real code pattern: function compute_dp(i, j, dp, prefix): if i > j return 0; min_cost = INF; for k in range(i, j+1): left = dp[i][k-1] if k-1 >= i else 0; right = dp[k+1][j] if k+1 <= j else 0; sum_freq = prefix[j] - (prefix[i-1] if i>0 else 0); cost = left + right + sum_freq; min_cost = min(min_cost, cost); return min_cost.

examples.trace: Interval [0,1] with keys 10,12. Try k=0: left = dp[0][-1] -> treat as 0 (empty), right = dp[1][1]=8, sum_freq = prefix[1]-0=42, cost=0+8+42=50. k=1: left = dp[0][0]=34, right = dp[2][1] -> treat as 0, sum_freq=42, cost=34+0+42=76. Minimum is 50.

Interval [1,2] with keys 12,20. Try k=1: left = dp[1][0]=0, right = dp[2][2]=50, sum_freq = prefix[2]-prefix[0]=92-34=58, cost=0+50+58=108. k=2: left = dp[1][1]=8, right = dp[3][2]=0, sum_freq=58, cost=8+0+58=66. Minimum is 66.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Decompose BST Cost into Subtree Costs
ladders.desc: Understand how total search cost for a BST can be broken down into costs of left and right subtrees plus a root penalty.
ladders.explanation: The core insight for the optimal BST DP is the recursive decomposition of the total search cost. For any BST, if we choose a root key k, the tree splits into a left subtree (containing keys less than k) and a right subtree (containing keys greater than k). The search cost for the entire tree can be expressed as: cost(tree) = cost(left subtree) + cost(right subtree) + sum of frequencies of all keys in the tree. Why? Consider the definition of search cost: for each key, cost = (depth + 1) × frequency. If we make k the root, every key in the tree moves one level deeper compared to its position in its subtree. Specifically, if a key has depth d in its subtree, its depth in the full tree is d+1. Thus, its contribution changes from (d+1)×freq to (d+2)×freq, an increase of freq. Summing over all keys, the total increase is the sum of all frequencies in the tree. Therefore, cost(full tree) = cost(left) + cost(right) + sum(freq). This additive property allows dynamic programming: we can compute the cost of a tree by combining costs of smaller subtrees. The sum of frequencies is independent of the tree structure and depends only on the set of keys, so we can precompute it. Intuition: This decomposition turns the problem into finding the root that minimizes the sum of subtree costs, since the sum(freq) is fixed for a given interval. It reduces the exponential search space of all BSTs to polynomial via DP. Time/space: This logic is conceptual, but enables O(n³) DP. Connection to L1: This logic is applied in the subroutine when computing cost for a root k. Common mistakes include misunderstanding the +sum(freq) term (it's not just the root's frequency) or incorrectly applying it to non-BST structures. Real code pattern: In pseudocode, when evaluating root k: cost = dp[i][k-1] + dp[k+1][j] + sum(freq[i:j+1]).

examples.trace: Tree with root 10: left subtree empty (cost 0), right subtree has key 12 (cost = (0+1)*8=8). Sum of frequencies = 34+8=42. Total cost = 0 + 8 + 42 = 50. Verify: In full tree, depth of 10 is 0, cost=34; depth of 12 is 1, cost=16; total=50.

Tree with root 12: left subtree has key 10 (cost=34), right subtree has key 20 (cost=50). Sum of frequencies = 34+8+50=92. Total cost = 34 + 50 + 92 = 176. Verify: In full tree, depth of 10 is 1 (cost=68), depth of 12 is 0 (cost=8), depth of 20 is 1 (cost=100); total=176.

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Sum of Frequencies for Any Interval
ladders.desc: Given an array of frequencies, efficiently compute the sum of elements from index i to j inclusive.
ladders.explanation: In the optimal BST DP, we frequently need the sum of frequencies for a contiguous interval [i, j]. Computing this sum naively by looping from i to j takes O(n) time per query, which would make the overall algorithm O(n⁴). To achieve O(n³), we precompute prefix sums. A prefix sum array 'prefix' of length n is built where prefix[k] = sum of frequencies from index 0 to k (inclusive). Then, the sum from i to j can be computed in O(1) time as: sum(i, j) = prefix[j] - (prefix[i-1] if i > 0 else 0). Intuition: This is a standard technique for range sum queries, leveraging the additive property of sums. It transforms a linear-time operation into constant time after O(n) preprocessing. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize prefix[0] = freq[0]. 2) For i from 1 to n-1, set prefix[i] = prefix[i-1] + freq[i]. 3) To query sum(i, j), return prefix[j] - (prefix[i-1] if i > 0 else 0). Time complexity: O(n) preprocessing, O(1) per query. Space: O(n) for prefix array. Connection to L2: This sum is the 'sum_freq' term in the cost decomposition. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices (e.g., using j-i+1 incorrectly), forgetting to handle i=0 case, or building prefix sums incorrectly. Real code pattern: prefix = [0]*n; prefix[0]=freq[0]; for i in range(1,n): prefix[i]=prefix[i-1]+freq[i]. Query: def sum_range(i,j): return prefix[j] - (prefix[i-1] if i>0 else 0).

examples.trace: Build prefix: prefix[0]=34, prefix[1]=34+8=42, prefix[2]=42+50=92. Query sum(0,2): prefix[2] - (prefix[-1] since i=0, use 0) = 92 - 0 = 92.
Prefix as above. Query sum(1,2): prefix[2] - prefix[0] = 92 - 34 = 58.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Access DP Table for Subtree Costs
ladders.desc: Retrieve or compute the minimum cost for a subtree given indices, handling empty subtrees.
ladders.explanation: In the DP algorithm, we frequently need the optimal cost for a subtree defined by indices [a, b]. This is stored in a 2D DP table where dp[a][b] represents the minimum search cost for keys from index a to b. However, we must handle cases where the subtree is empty (i.e., a > b). For an empty subtree, the cost is 0 because there are no keys to search. In practice, when accessing dp[i][k-1] for the left subtree or dp[k+1][j] for the right subtree, we may have indices that are out of bounds (e.g., k-1 < i or k+1 > j). We treat these as empty subtrees with cost 0. Intuition: This is a boundary condition that ensures the recursion terminates correctly. It aligns with the base case in DP where dp[i][j] for i > j is 0. Step-by-step: When computing cost for root k in interval [i, j], let left_cost = dp[i][k-1] if k-1 >= i else 0. Let right_cost = dp[k+1][j] if k+1 <= j else 0. This check prevents array index errors and correctly assigns zero cost. Time/space: O(1) per access. Connection to L3: This operation is used after computing sum of frequencies to get the total cost. Common mistakes include not checking bounds leading to index errors, or incorrectly setting cost to a non-zero value for empty subtrees. Real code pattern: In a loop over k: left = dp[i][k-1] if k-1 >= i else 0; right = dp[k+1][j] if k+1 <= j else 0.

examples.trace: For k=0: left subtree indices [0, -1] -> k-1=-1 which is < i=0, so left=0. Right subtree indices [1,1] -> k+1=1 which is <= j=1, so right=dp[1][1]=8.
For k=1: left subtree indices [0,0] -> k-1=0 >= i=0, so left=dp[0][0]=34. Right subtree indices [2,1] -> k+1=2 > j=1, so right=0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Search Tree (BST) Structure
ladders.desc: Understand the basic properties of a Binary Search Tree and how keys are organized.
ladders.explanation: A Binary Search Tree (BST) is a binary tree data structure where each node has a key, and for any node, all keys in its left subtree are less than the node's key, and all keys in its right subtree are greater. This property enables efficient search, insertion, and deletion operations. In the context of the optimal BST problem, we are given a set of keys (usually sorted in increasing order) and their associated search frequencies. The goal is to arrange these keys into a BST to minimize the total search cost. The tree structure affects the depth of each key: keys closer to the root have smaller depth, reducing their contribution to the cost if they have high frequency. Intuition: By placing high-frequency keys near the root, we can lower the total cost. The BST property restricts how keys can be arranged: for a given root, the left subtree must contain only smaller keys, and the right subtree only larger keys. This constraint allows dynamic programming by considering contiguous intervals of the sorted key array. Step-by-step: Given sorted keys [k0, k1, ..., kn-1], any BST must have a root k_r. Then, keys k0 to k_{r-1} go to the left subtree, and keys k_{r+1} to k_{n-1} go to the right subtree. This recursive splitting is the basis for the DP approach. Time/space: This is a conceptual foundation; no specific complexity. Connection to L4: The DP table accesses correspond to costs for contiguous key intervals, which are valid BST subtrees due to this property. Common mistakes include not sorting keys first, or misunderstanding the BST property (e.g., allowing equal keys). Real code pattern: Ensure keys are sorted. In pseudocode, assume keys are sorted ascending.

examples.trace: Sorted keys: [10,12,20]. If root is 12, left subtree must contain keys less than 12: only 10. Right subtree must contain keys greater than 12: only 20. This forms a valid BST.
Root 10: left subtree empty (no keys less than 10). Right subtree contains keys greater than 10: [12,20]. Within right subtree, root could be 12 or 20, following same rule.
